issue_info.title: Unsupported __class__ attr for tuple or list cause graph break in torch.compile

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Using __class__ for tuple and list in code causes graph break for torch.compile.

The following code shows the tuple example showing the error:

```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
def fn(x):
  touched_outputs = []
  for v in x:
    y = v.__class__(torch.zeros(2, 2))
    touched_output = v * y
    touched_outputs.append(touched_output)
  return x.__class__(touched_outputs)
  
compiled_fn = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True)
data1 = torch.ones(2, 2)
data2 = torch.ones(2, 2)
output = compiled_fn((data1, data2))
print(output)
```

The above case shows the following error:

```
torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported: call_method TupleVariable() __class__ [ListVariable(length=2)] {}

from user code:
   File "/home/haifchen/working/test/test-class-method.py", line 104, in fn
    return x.__class__(touched_outputs)
```

Using list and namedtuple have similar issues. The following is the list example showing the error:

```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def fn(x):
  touched_outputs = []
  for v in x:
    y = v.__class__(torch.zeros(2, 2))
    touched_output = v * y
    touched_outputs.append(touched_output)
  return x.__class__(touched_outputs)
  
compiled_fn = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True)

data1 = torch.ones(2, 2)
data2 = torch.ones(2, 2)

output = compiled_fn([data1, data2])

print(output)
```
The following is the named tuple example showing error:

```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import collections

def fn(x):
  touched_outputs = []
  for v in x:
    y = v.__class__(torch.zeros(2, 2))
    touched_output = v * y
    touched_outputs.append(touched_output)
  return x.__class__(*touched_outputs)
  
compiled_fn = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True)

data1 = torch.ones(2, 2)
data2 = torch.ones(2, 2)

p = collections.namedtuple("Point", ["x", "y"])
t = p(data1, data2)

output = compiled_fn(t)

print(output)
```

issue_info.title: `tree_map_only` does not support closure when compile

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

There is `torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported: reconstruct: NewCellVariable()` when torch.compile a function with `tree_map_only` on closures. A minimal repro:

```python
import torch
from torch.utils._pytree import tree_map_only

# ===========
# Example 1
# ===========

class GroupedTensor:
    def __init__(self, a, b):
        self.a = a
        self.b = b

    def __getitem__(self, index):
        mapped_attributes = tree_map_only(
            torch.Tensor,
            lambda x: x[index],
            (self.a, self.b),
        )
        return mapped_attributes

def getitem(grouped_tensor: GroupedTensor, index: torch.Tensor):
    return grouped_tensor[index]


grouped_tensor = GroupedTensor(torch.randn(4, 2, 5), torch.randn(4, 2,5,5))
compiled_fn = torch.compile(getitem, fullgraph=True)
compiled_fn(grouped_tensor, torch.tensor([2]))

# ===========
# Example 2
# ===========
def f(a, b, index):
    def call(index):
        mapped_attributes = tree_map_only(
            torch.Tensor,
            lambda x: x[index],
            (a, b),
        )
        return mapped_attributes
    return call(index)

a = torch.randn(4, 2, 5)
b = torch.randn(4, 2,5,5)
compiled_fn = torch.compile(f, fullgraph=True)
compiled_fn(a, b, torch.tensor([2]))
```

Error log:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/data/users/boyuan/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/codegen.py", line 231, in __call__
    self.call_reconstruct(value)
  File "/data/users/boyuan/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/codegen.py", line 84, in call_reconstruct
    res = value.reconstruct(self)
  File "/data/users/boyuan/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/variables/base.py", line 271, in reconstruct
    raise NotImplementedError
NotImplementedError
```

issue_info.title: Map with multiple arguments not supported in Dynamo and causes graph breaks

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: empathy-day
labels.description: Label for issues from user empathy days

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

```
import torch
torch.set_default_device('cuda')

def f(a, b):
    return (a[0] * b[0] + a[1] * b[1])

x = [torch.randn(5) for _ in range(3)]
y = [torch.randn(5) for _ in range(3)]
torch.compile(lambda x, y: map(f, x, y), fullgraph=True)(x, y)
```

```
torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported: invalid handler args <bound method BuiltinVariable.call_map of BuiltinVariable()> [UserFunctionVariable(), ListVariable(length=3), ListVariable(length=3)] {}
```

### Versions

N/A

issue_info.title: torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: AttributeError: 'MutableMappingVariable' object has no attribute 'items'

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

deepspeed hook parameters with `ZeROOrderedDict`, which was wrapped as `MutableMappingVariable`, but it has no `items` attribute.

```log
  File "/home/jianma/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/variables/nn_module.py", line 932, in collect_parameters
    params_list = get_current_parameters(module_var)
  File "/home/jianma/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/variables/nn_module.py", line 917, in get_current_parameters
    params_dict = module_var.var_getattr(tx, "_parameters").realize().items
torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: AttributeError: 'MutableMappingVariable' object has no attribute 'items'
```

```python
import os
os.environ["TORCH_LOGS"] = "+dynamo"
# os.environ["TORCHDYNAMO_INLINE_INBUILT_NN_MODUES"] = "0"

import torch
# torch._dynamo.config.inline_inbuilt_nn_modules = False

from collections import OrderedDict


DEVICE = "cpu"


class ParametersModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear1 = torch.nn.Linear(2, 2)
        self.scale = torch.nn.Parameter(torch.randn(2, 2))
        self.scale_dup = self.scale

    def forward(self, x):
        counter = 0
        for param in self.parameters():
            counter += 1
        return x * self.scale * counter


class ZeROOrderedDict(OrderedDict):
    def __init__(self, parent_module=None, *args, **kwargs):
        """A replacement for ``collections.OrderedDict`` to detect external ZeRO params.

        Args:
            parent_module (``collections.OrderedDict``): the collection to replace
        """

        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self._parent_module = parent_module

    def __getitem__(self, key):
        param = super().__getitem__(key)

        # Params can be registered as None (e.g., bias)
        if param is None:
            return param
        
       # do something here
        return param


def inject_parameters(module, cls):
    for module in module.modules():
        if cls == ZeROOrderedDict:
            new_param = cls(parent_module=module)
        else:
            new_param = cls()

        for key, param in module._parameters.items():
            new_param[key] = param
        module._parameters = new_param


model = ParametersModule()

inject_parameters(model, ZeROOrderedDict)

model= model.to(DEVICE)

model = torch.compile(model, backend="inductor", fullgraph=False)

x = torch.ones(2).to(DEVICE)
y = model(x)
print(y)

```

issue_info.title: [inline-inbuilt-nn-modules] Unlimited recompiles because the nn.Module is going out of scope

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

~~~
import torch

class Mod1(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.lin = torch.nn.Linear(5, 5)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.lin(x)

def fn(x, mod):
    return mod(x)

opt_mod = torch.compile(fn, backend="eager")
for i in range(100):
    opt_mod(torch.randn(5, 5), Mod1())
~~~

This keeps on recompiling, even though our cache size limit is 8.

The reason is that `Mod1()` is ephemeral. As soon as it dies, the Dynamo cache entry gets invalidated and deleted. So, at the end of each for loop iteration, the cache size is effectively zero.

This can be solved by turning on `torch._dynamo.config.inline_inbuilt_nn_modules`. If we can't wait for that flag, we might need to disable the optimization that eagerly deletes the cache line.

### Error logs

_No response_

### Minified repro

_No response_

### Versions

NA

issue_info.title: Mutating global variable during inlining a function from imported module breaks in dynamo
issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

This repro requires two files, test_import.py, and repro.py. test_import.py consists of simple functions which mutate a global variable (module scoped). When those functions are called from repro.py, dynamo attempts to add the PythonModule variable to side effects twice, causing the error:

```
AssertionError: PythonModuleVariable(<module 'test_import' from '/data/users/mlazos/test/test_import.py'>) is already tracked for mutation. This could be because you are not using VariableBuilder to construct the variable tracker. Source of new object: GlobalSource(global_name='__import_test_import'). Source of previously tracked object: GlobalSource(global_name='__import_test_import').

from user code:
   File "/data/users/mlazos/test/test_mutate_flag.py", line 8, in fn
    test_import.set_falg_false()
  File "/data/users/mlazos/test/test_import.py", line 12, in set_falg_false
    global_flag = False

Set TORCH_LOGS="+dynamo" and TORCHDYNAMO_VERBOSE=1 for more information
```

file: test_import.py
contents:
```py
global_flag = False


def set_flag_true():
    global global_flag
    global_flag = True

def set_falg_false():
    global global_flag
    global_flag = False
```

file: repro.py
contents:
```py
import test_import
import torch


@torch.compile()
def fn(x):
    test_import.set_flag_true()
    test_import.set_falg_false()
    return x + 1

fn(torch.ones(2, 2))
```

### Versions

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

repro command: ```pytest -s test/distributed/tensor/parallel/test_tp_examples_compile.py```

```
# Copyright (c) Meta Platforms, Inc. and affiliates
# Owner(s): ["oncall: distributed"]

import torch
from torch.distributed._tensor import DeviceMesh, Shard
from torch.distributed.tensor.parallel import (
    ColwiseParallel,
    parallelize_module,
    RowwiseParallel,
)
from torch.testing._internal.common_utils import run_tests
from torch.testing._internal.distributed._tensor.common_dtensor import (
    DTensorTestBase,
    MLPModule,
    NUM_DEVICES,
    with_comms,
)
import logging
torch._logging.set_logs(dynamo = logging.DEBUG)
torch._dynamo.config.verbose = True


c10d_functional = torch.ops.c10d_functional


# simplifed tests from test/distributed/tensor/parallel/test_tp_examples.py
class DistTensorParallelExampleTest(DTensorTestBase):
    @with_comms
    def test_mlp_training(self, is_seq_parallel=False):
        inp_size = [8, 10]
        # Ensure all tp ranks have same input.
        rng_seed = self.rank if is_seq_parallel else 0
        torch.manual_seed(rng_seed)
        inp = torch.rand(*inp_size, device=self.device_type)
        model = MLPModule(self.device_type)
        device_mesh = DeviceMesh(
            self.device_type,
            torch.arange(0, NUM_DEVICES),
        )
        parallelize_plan = {
            "net1": ColwiseParallel(input_layouts=Shard(0))
            if is_seq_parallel
            else ColwiseParallel(),
            "net2": RowwiseParallel(output_layouts=Shard(0))
            if is_seq_parallel
            else RowwiseParallel(),
        }
        model_tp = parallelize_module(model, device_mesh, parallelize_plan)
        # model_tp = torch.compile(model_tp)
        model_tp.compile()
        output = model_tp(inp)
        output.sum().backward()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_tests()
```

issue_info.title: [Compiled_autograd] running deepspeed Zero3 failed for torch.compile with compiled_autograd

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: module: compiled autograd
labels.description: compiled_autograd

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

When running a simple model including torch.nn.Linear using deepspeed zero3 with torch.compile and [compiled_autograd](https://github.com/pytorch/tutorials/blob/main/intermediate_source/compiled_autograd_tutorial.rst). An error occurs:
> torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: RuntimeError: attempting to assign a gradient of size '[10]' to a tensor of size '[0]'. Please ensure that the gradient and the tensor are the same size

This is another error that relates to deepspeed zero3 grad-related hooks (for all-reduce) when using compiled autograd.
It happens after compiled autograd graph is generated and during that compiled autograd graph is traced through dynamo.

From my previous issue [#140091](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/140091), @xmfan have already given me some hint that the error is caused by parameter offloading. Which means **when dynamo traces the grad-related hook, the param is still offloaded(its size is(0,)).**
So it's a good idea to graph break the code by adding `@torch._dynamo.disable` in [grad-related hooks](https://github.com/microsoft/DeepSpeed/blob/master/deepspeed/runtime/zero/stage3.py#L1176).
```python
@torch._dynamo.disable
def reduce_partition_and_remove_grads(...)
      ...
```
It works for current deepspeed code. But after applying a robust `param.register_post_accumulate_grad_hook(..)` as https://github.com/microsoft/DeepSpeed/pull/6773. The error reappears. 

### Investigation
The error: "torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: RuntimeError: attempting to assign a gradient of size '[10]' to a tensor of size '[0]'. Please ensure that the gradient and the tensor are the same size"
It happens when dynamo traces generated compiled autograd graph.

Below is deepspeed_reproducer_cpu2.py
```python
import torch
import deepspeed
from deepspeed.accelerator import get_accelerator
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

class Net(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(32, 10) 

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.fc1(x)
        return x

compile_kwargs = {"dynamic": False}
device = torch.device('cpu')

model = Net()
model.to(device)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.001, momentum=0.9)
model_engine, optimizer, *_ = deepspeed.initialize(
    model=model,
    model_parameters=model.parameters(),
    optimizer=optimizer,
    config="./deepspeed_config.json",
)
# torch_compile
model_engine.compile(
    compile_kwargs=compile_kwargs
)

for epoch in range(100):
    with torch._dynamo.compiled_autograd.enable(
                torch.compile(backend=get_accelerator().get_compile_backend(), **compile_kwargs)):
        inputs=torch.randn([8,32], requires_grad=True)
        labels=torch.ones([8])
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        # forward + backward + optimize
        outputs = model_engine(inputs)
        loss = criterion(outputs.float(), labels.long())
        model_engine.backward(loss)
        model_engine.step()
print("Finished Training")
```

```json
}
```

issue_info.title: Implementing caching under `torch.compile` cause error

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

```
import torch

cached = {}
def g(x):
    return x.sin().sin().sin().sin()

@torch.compile(dynamic=False)
def f(x):
    if x.shape not in cached:
        cached[x.shape] = g(x)
    return x + cached[x.shape]

f(torch.randn(10))
f(torch.randn(10))
```

issue_info.title: Dynamo inlining errors with some calls to nested functions that use captured variables

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Repro:

```python
import torch

# We must wrap the whole thing into a function to reproduce the error.
def test():
    x = torch.ones(1)

    def fn():
        def inner():
            return x + 2
        return inner

    @torch.compile
    def start():
        fn_inner = fn()
        res = fn_inner()
        return res, fn_inner

    start()

test()
```

issue_info.title: [Dynamo] Handle tensor attributes

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:

```
    torch._dynamo.config.suppress_errors = True
```

### Minified repro

```python
import torch
import torch._dynamo as dynamo


dynamo.reset()

def toy_example():
    def add_one(x):
        return x + 1

    t = torch.nn.Parameter(torch.tensor(1.))
    t.add_one = add_one
    return t.add_one(t)

compiled_fn = torch.compile(toy_example, backend="inductor")

r1 = compiled_fn()
print(f"r1 = {r1}")
```

issue_info.body:

The following testcase can reproduce this issue:

```
import torch

def func(x):
    # return torch.add(x, 1).numpy()
    return torch.Tensor.numpy(torch.add(x, 1))

x = torch.randn(32, 3, 64, 64)
compiled_func = torch.compile(func)

with torch.no_grad():
    compiled_func(x)
```

issue_info.title: [dynamo] Speculation log diverges when Lazy Module is used in a certain way

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

The following logic interacts in a way that causes speculation log to diverge:
1. When we construct a `VariableTracker` for an `nn.Module` instance, the following predicate (partially) determines whether it becomes a `NNModuleVariable` or `UnspecializedNNModuleVariable`; note the `if is_lazy_module(obj)` check.
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/2b7c7a20b9c0e8e7f2773ffc5c9f79c3cae2070b/torch/_dynamo/variables/builder.py#L1393
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/2b7c7a20b9c0e8e7f2773ffc5c9f79c3cae2070b/torch/_dynamo/mutation_guard.py#L95-L103

2. When Dynamo traces call to `*NNModuleVariable`, if the underlying module instance is a lazy module, it'll run its initialization first, which causes the instance to become a non-lazy module.
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/2b7c7a20b9c0e8e7f2773ffc5c9f79c3cae2070b/torch/_dynamo/variables/nn_module.py#L54-L79

3. When tracing a `mod(input)` call for `NNModuleVariable`, Dynamo usually doesn't inline the `forward` method (we just track it as an opaque call in the fx graph). However, for `UnspecializedNNModuleVariable`, Dynamo always inline the `forward` method.
- `NNModuleVariable.call_function`:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/2b7c7a20b9c0e8e7f2773ffc5c9f79c3cae2070b/torch/_dynamo/variables/nn_module.py#L417-L424
- `UnspecializedNNModuleVariable.call_function`:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/2b7c7a20b9c0e8e7f2773ffc5c9f79c3cae2070b/torch/_dynamo/variables/nn_module.py#L902-L904

Dynamo restart (e.g., due to graph break) and (1), (2) together could cause a lazy module instance to be modeled as different module variables across 2 tracings; then (3) causes Dynamo tracing to diverge from the speculation log, which was gathered before the graph break.

```verbatim
- Actual: /Users/ryanguo99/Documents/work/pytorch/torch/nn/modules/linear.py:125 (CALL at ip=8)

There are two usual reasons why this may have occured:
- When Dynamo analysis restarted, the second run took a different path than
  the first.  If this occurred, the previous instruction is the critical instruction that
  behaved differently.
- Speculation entries are only added under certain conditions (as seen in
  step()), e.g., there must exist operators in the graph; those conditions may
  have changed on restart.

do this for graph breaks, you will infinite loop).

    import torch._dynamo
    torch._dynamo.config.suppress_errors = True
```

### Minified repro

Just run `python test.py` on the following program

```python
import torch

def test_lazy_module():

    class ModWithOneLazyLinear(torch.nn.Module):
        def __init__(self) -> None:
            super().__init__()
            self.layer = torch.nn.LazyLinear(8)

        def forward(self, x):
            return self.layer(x)

    # This allows us to restart tracing without clearing speculation log
    def id_and_graph_break(x):
        torch._dynamo.graph_break()
        return x

    @torch.compile()
    def foo(mod, x):
        # We'll trace this `mod(x)` call 2 times (because of the manual graph
        # break right after it)
        #
        # - 1st time the `mod.layer` would be treated as an `NNModuleVariable`
        # (because it's a `LazyLinear`), so we won't trace into its call method,
        # and would leave it as an opaque fx call to the linear layer.
        #
        # NOTE that as part of tracing `mod.layer(x)` we'd hit
        # `initialize_lazy_module`, which changes the underlying `mod.layer`
        # from a LazyLinear to a Linear instance.
        #
        # - 2nd time the `mod.layer` would be treated as an
        # `UnspecializedNNModuleVariable` (because it's now a `Linear`), and
        # we'd trace into the linear layer rather than keeping it as a single fx
        # call. This breaks speculation log.
        res = mod(x)
        res2 = id_and_graph_break(res)
        return res

    x = torch.ones(10, 3)
    mod = ModWithOneLazyLinear()
    foo(mod, x)

test_lazy_module()
```

### Versions

Python 3.12.5, at 2b7c7a20.

issue_info.title: torch.compile fullgraph=True is failing for GPTJ model for toy_backend

labels.name: feature
labels.description: A request for a proper, new feature.

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

```
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, GPTJForCausalLM

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("hf-internal-testing/tiny-random-gptj")
model = GPTJForCausalLM.from_pretrained("hf-internal-testing/tiny-random-gptj")
inputs = tokenizer("Hello, my dog is cute", return_tensors="pt")

outputs = model(**inputs, labels=inputs["input_ids"])
input_ids = inputs["input_ids"]
out= model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=True, temperature=0.9, max_length=200)

import torch._dynamo
from torch._functorch.aot_autograd import aot_module_simplified
from torch._dynamo.backends.debugging import ExplainWithBackend

def toy_backend(gm, sample_inputs):
    def my_compiler(gm, sample_inputs):
        # <implement your compiler here>
        print("AOTAutograd produced a fx Graph in Aten IR:")
        # gm.print_readable()
        # gm.graph.print_tabular()
        # print(len(sample_inputs))
        return gm.forward

    # Invoke AOTAutograd
    return aot_module_simplified(
        gm,
        sample_inputs,
        fw_compiler=my_compiler
    )

torch._dynamo.reset()
fn = torch.compile(backend=toy_backend, dynamic=True,fullgraph=True)(model.generate)
out = fn(input_ids, do_sample=True, temperature=0.9, max_length=200)


```

issue_info.title: torch.compile can supports load_state_dict

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

I want to use torch.compile on the init model, and then reload the model. But I used load_state_dict not come into effect. But the weights have been updated. 
But it works when I update the weights manually. I don't know why. Can you help me.

### Error logs

_No response_

### Minified repro

You can comment line24~line28, and this will run successfully

```python
import torch

class MyModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.lin = torch.nn.Linear(5, 5)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.lin(x)

mod = MyModule().cuda()
mod.forward = torch.compile(mod.forward, fullgraph=True)
input = torch.ones(1, 5).cuda()
with torch.no_grad():
    print('origin output: ', mod(input))

state_dict = mod.state_dict()
torch.save(state_dict, "test_weight.pth")
mod.lin.weight.data.fill_(0)
mod.lin.bias.data.fill_(0)
with torch.no_grad():
    print('init output: ', mod(input))

# This approach will fail 
state_dict = torch.load('test_weight.pth')
mod.load_state_dict(state_dict, assign=True)
with torch.no_grad():
    print('reload output: ', mod(input))

# This approach will success
state_dict = torch.load('test_weight.pth')
mod.lin.weight.data = state_dict['lin.weight']
mod.lin.bias.data = state_dict['lin.bias']
with torch.no_grad():
    print('reload output 2: ', mod(input))
```

issue_info.title: cache entry not found when using torch.compile inside a function

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Here is the code to reproduce:

```python
import torch
from torch import nn

class Model(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = nn.Linear(128, 128)

    def forward(self, x):
        out = self.linear(x) + 1
        return out

code = Model.forward.__code__

def bytecode_hook(old, new):
    assert old is code

torch._dynamo.convert_frame.register_bytecode_hook(bytecode_hook)

def main():
    model = Model()
    model = torch.compile(model=model)
    data = torch.randn(64, 128)
    loss = model(data)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    from torch._dynamo.eval_frame import _debug_get_cache_entry_list

    # cannot find cache entry
    main()
    print(_debug_get_cache_entry_list(Model.forward.__code__))

    # can find cache entry
    model = Model()
    model = torch.compile(model=model)
    data = torch.randn(64, 128)
    loss = model(data)

    print(_debug_get_cache_entry_list(Model.forward.__code__))
```

The output is:

```text
[]
[<torch._C._dynamo.eval_frame._CacheEntry object at 0x16e0877b0>]
```

Note that I use a bytecode hook to make sure `Model.forward.__code__` is the code object we compile.

When I call the code from a function (`main()`), surprisingly I cannot find any cache entry.

When I directly run these code outside of the function (i.e. in the global scope), the code behaves normally, and I can get the code cache entry.

`Could not guard on data-dependent expression u0 > 2 (unhinted: u0 > 2).  (Size-like symbols: u0)`. This error happens when fullgraph=True during dynamo tracing: we call `compute_elementwise_output_logical_to_physical_perm` to determine output strides of the matmul, which can swap dimensions for reasons I didn't really understand: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/d6481333ad3371825be3fd5fda6d7eae1fad7345/torch/_prims_common/__init__.py#L491-L513

Any of these checks could error with unbacked symints, and in the repro below, we mark certain shapes as unbacked in order to avoid zero-one specialization, so we error when performing the last shape check: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/d6481333ad3371825be3fd5fda6d7eae1fad7345/torch/_prims_common/__init__.py#L507-L509


Repro:
```python
import torch

@torch.compile(backend="eager", fullgraph=True)
def fn(aot6_sub_58, aot6_mul_170):
    aot6_unsqueeze_14 = torch.ops.aten.unsqueeze.default(aot6_mul_170, 1)
    return torch.ops.aten.mul.Tensor(aot6_sub_58, aot6_unsqueeze_14)

aot6_sub_58 = torch.randn(2, 1)
torch._dynamo.decorators.mark_unbacked(aot6_sub_58, 1)
aot6_mul_170 = torch.randn(2)

fn(aot6_sub_58, aot6_mul_170)
```
### Versions

main

issue_info.title: [dynamo] Populate code_parts in C++ guard_manager for depyf

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

This code will fail on the main branch:

```python
import torch

class LinearModel(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(LinearModel, self).__init__()
        self.linear = torch.nn.Linear(1, 1)
    def forward(self, x):
        def forward_function(x):
            return self.linear(x)
        return forward_function(x)

model = LinearModel()
model = torch.compile(model, backend='eager')

x = torch.tensor([[1.0]])

torch._dynamo.config.enable_cpp_guard_manager = False
model(x)
```

issue_info.title: [dynamo] DAC: 'AudioSignal' object has no attribute 'sample_rate'

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Running the compress/decompress stage of [DAC](https://github.com/descriptinc/descript-audio-codec) leads to several errors `'AudioSignal' object has no attribute 'sample_rate'`. Here AudioSignal is a custom class storing a mixture of tensor data (e.g. audio_data) and other numerical attributes, including sample_rate (integer).

### Error logs

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/data/users/pianpwk/pytorch/descript-audio-codec/test-dac.py", line 45, in <module>
    x = _compress(signal)
  File "/data/users/pianpwk/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 432, in _fn
    return fn(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/data/users/pianpwk/pytorch/descript-audio-codec/test-dac.py", line 43, in _compress
    return model.compress(signal)
  File "/data/users/pianpwk/pytorch/torch/utils/_contextlib.py", line 115, in decorate_context
    return func(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/data/users/pianpwk/pytorch/descript-audio-codec/dac/model/base.py", line 159, in compress
    self.eval()
  File "/data/users/pianpwk/pytorch/descript-audio-codec/dac/model/base.py", line 163, in torch_dynamo_resume_in_compress_at_159
    audio_signal = audio_signal.clone()
  File "/home/pianpwk/.conda/envs/pytorch-3.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/audiotools/core/audio_signal.py", line 641, in clone
    clone = type(self)(
  File "/home/pianpwk/.conda/envs/pytorch-3.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/audiotools/core/audio_signal.py", line 122, in __init__
    def __init__(
  File "/home/pianpwk/.conda/envs/pytorch-3.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/audiotools/core/audio_signal.py", line 1066, in stft_params
    default_win_len = int(2 ** (np.ceil(np.log2(0.032 * self.sample_rate))))
AttributeError: 'AudioSignal' object has no attribute 'sample_rate'
```

### Minified repro

```
# built from source: https://github.com/descriptinc/descript-audio-codec/tree/main
import dac
import torch
from audiotools import AudioSignal

# Download a model
model_path = dac.utils.download(model_type="44khz")
model = dac.DAC.load(model_path)
model.to('cuda')

# Load audio signal file
# 4ch.wav is located here: https://www.mmsp.ece.mcgill.ca/Documents/AudioFormats/WAVE/Samples/SoundCardAttrition/4ch.wav
signal = AudioSignal('4ch.wav')
signal._audio_data = signal._audio_data[:, :1, :]
# Encode audio signal as one long file
# (may run out of GPU memory on long files)
signal.to(model.device)

@torch.compile
def _compress(signal):
    return model.compress(signal)
x = _compress(signal)

# Decompress it back to an AudioSignal
@torch.compile
def _decompress(x):
    return model.decompress(x)
y = _decompress(x)
```

issue_info.title: _functional_collectives.all_gather_into_tensor cannot compile in aot_module_simplified

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

torch.distributed._functional_collectives.all_gather_tensor can work great in make_fx，but it cannot work in aot_module_simplified.
It doesn't work because assert self.is_contiguous() exists in _functional_collectives.all_gather_into_tensor api.
The script I'm running is：
```
import torch
import os
from torch.fx.experimental.proxy_tensor import make_fx
import torch.distributed as dist
import torch.distributed._functional_collectives as funcol
import torch.multiprocessing as mp
from torch._functorch.aot_autograd import aot_module_simplified
from typing import Any, Dict, Iterator, List, Optional, Tuple, Union

def toy_compiler(gm: torch.fx.GraphModule, example_inputs: List[torch.Tensor]):
    print(gm.graph)
    return gm

def toy_backend(gm: torch.fx.GraphModule, example_inputs: List[torch.Tensor]):
    return aot_module_simplified(gm, example_inputs,fw_compiler=toy_compiler)

class MyModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = funcol.all_gather_tensor(x, 0, group=[0,1],tag='test1')
        return x

def example(rank, world_size):
    dist.init_process_group("gloo", rank=rank, world_size=world_size)
    mod = MyModule()
    opt_mod = torch.compile(mod, dynamic=True, fullgraph=True, backend=toy_backend)
    x = torch.randn(10, 100)
    out = opt_mod(x)
    print(out)


def main():
    world_size = 2
    mp.spawn(example,
             args=(world_size, ),
             nprocs=world_size,
             join=True)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    os.environ["MASTER_ADDR"] = "localhost"
    os.environ["MASTER_PORT"] = "29516"
    main()
```

issue_info.title: Graph break when there is backward hook registered for sub module

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: module: compiled autograd
labels.description: compiled_autograd

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

When there is a backward hook registered for sub module, there are graph breaks caused by Unsupported: call_method NNModuleVariable() _get_backward_hooks [] {}

The following is the test case showing this this problem:

```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn


def hook_fn(module, input, output):
    # do anything you like
    return None


class SubM(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc = nn.Linear(2, 2)
        self.register_full_backward_hook(hook_fn)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc(x)
    
class M(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(2, 2)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(2, 2)
        self.predictions = SubM()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.predictions(x)
        x = self.fc1(x)
        return self.fc2(x)

model = M()

opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, momentum=0.9)

data = torch.ones(2, requires_grad = True)
labels = torch.ones(2, requires_grad = True).long()

def train(m):
    m.train()
    m = torch.compile(m, fullgraph=True)
    train_one_epoch(m)


def train_one_epoch(m):
    opt.zero_grad(True)
    output = m(data)
    loss = (output - labels).sum()
    loss.backward()
    opt.step()

train(model)
```

Run the above problem shows the following error:

```
torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported: call_method NNModuleVariable() _get_backward_hooks [] {}

from user code:
   File "/home/haifchen/working/test/test-compile-get_backward_hooks.py", line 204, in forward
    x = self.predictions(x)
  File "/home/haifchen/working/envs/env_test_pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1574, in _call_impl
    full_backward_hooks, non_full_backward_hooks = self._get_backward_hooks()
```

Even after fixed the _get_backward_hooks error  by adding _get_backward_hooks implementation in NNModuleVariable call_method, there are more issues coming from trying compiling the _call_impl_ method of the sub module with backward hooks. For example, the following error may show after fixing _get_backward_hooks:

```
torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported: module-level backwards hooks require compiled autograd

from user code:
   File "/home/haifchen/working/test/test-compile-get_backward_hooks.py", line 202, in forward
    x = self.predictions(x)
  File "/home/haifchen/working/envs/env_test_pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1600, in _call_impl
    bw_hook = hooks.BackwardHook(self, full_backward_hooks, backward_pre_hooks)
```


### Versions

issue_info.title: aten::bucketize.Scalar doesn't work in compile mode

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: internal ramp-up task
labels.description: Tasks that are suitable for new folks w/ high-touch guidance from senior PyTorch folks

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

aten::bucketize.Scalar works in eager mode, but it can't run compile mode. Scalar is passed to https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/_refs/__init__.py#L5901 and it tries to call tensor.shape() on it.

aten::bucketize.Tensor works as expected in both modes.

### Error logs

TorchRuntimeError: Failed running call_function <built-in method bucketize of type object at 0x7c6c17281760>(*(6.3, FakeTensor(..., size=(s0,))), **{}):
'float' object has no attribute 'shape'

from user code:
   File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/torch/_dynamo/external_utils.py", line 36, in inner
    return fn(*args, **kwargs)

Set TORCH_LOGS="+dynamo" and TORCHDYNAMO_VERBOSE=1 for more information


You can suppress this exception and fall back to eager by setting:
    import torch._dynamo
    torch._dynamo.config.suppress_errors = True

### Minified repro

import torch
fn = torch.bucketize
fn(6.3, torch.tensor([1.4, 3.2, 5.1, 8.9]))

fn = torch.compile(fn)
fn(6.3, torch.tensor([1.4, 3.2, 5.1, 8.9]))

issue_info.title: `setattr(MutableMappingVariable, ...)` not supported

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

The compiler complains that setting attributes of a `MutableMappingVariable` is not allowed.

Running the following code requires installing `tensordict`.

```python
from tensordict import from_module, TensorDictParams, TensorDict
import torch.nn

module = torch.nn.Module()
module.params = torch.nn.Parameter(torch.ones(3))
params2 = TensorDict({"params": torch.ones(3) + 1})

@torch.compile(fullgraph=True)
def func(z, params2):
    with params2.to_module(module):
        out = z + module.params
    return out 

print(func(torch.zeros(()), params2))
```

Related: https://github.com/pytorch/tensordict/pull/1100#issuecomment-2491805272

### Error logs

issue_info.title: [inline_inbuilt_nn_modules] Custom `ModuleDict.__getitem__(key: tuple)` produces a graph break

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: dynamo-must-fix
labels.description: These bugs affect TorchDynamo reliability.

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

#97932 enabled TorchDynamo to support modules with custom `__getitem__`. However, if the key is a tuple, it produces a graph break due to this assertion https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/v2.1.0/torch/_dynamo/variables/nn_module.py#L545.

```python
import torch
from torch_geometric.nn.module_dict import ModuleDict

class SomeModel(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.module_dict = ModuleDict({
            ("author", "writes", "paper"): torch.nn.Linear(1, 1),
        })

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.module_dict[("author", "writes", "paper")](x)
        return x

model = torch.compile(SomeModel())
model(torch.randn(100, 1))
```

`torch_geometric.nn.module_dict.ModuleDict` is defined at https://github.com/pyg-team/pytorch_geometric/blob/2.4.0/torch_geometric/nn/module_dict.py

issue_info.title: [inline-inbuilt-nn-modules][dynamo] AssertionError for custom iterable nn.Module

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: enhancement
labels.description: Not as big of a feature, but technically not a bug. Should be easy to fix

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Using a custom iterable nn.Module causes an AssertionError when dynamo is enabled.
The use case here is creating an equivalent of nn.ParameterList, but for buffers, like in [this issue](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/37386) or [this question](https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/why-no-nn-bufferlist-like-function-for-registered-buffer-tensor/18884/3)
The code hits an assertion in __dynamo/variables/nn_module.py_ line 99 (in _unpack_var_sequence_), which seems to explicitly disallow custom iterable modules.
Verified in nighly 2023.06.17

### Minified repro

```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class BufferList(nn.Module):
    """
    Similar to nn.ParameterList, but for buffers
    """

    def __init__(self, buffers=None):
        super(BufferList, self).__init__()
        if buffers is not None:
            self.extend(buffers)

    def extend(self, buffers):
        offset = len(self)
        for i, buffer in enumerate(buffers):
            self.register_buffer(str(offset + i), buffer)
        return self

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self._buffers)

    def __iter__(self):
        return iter(self._buffers.values())

class Containing(nn.Module):
  def __init__(self, buflist):
    super().__init__()
    self.buflist = buflist
  def forward(self):
    for x in self.buflist:
      print(x.shape)

print(torch.__version__) #2.1.0.dev20230617+cu118
x = Containing(BufferList((torch.rand((5,5)), torch.rand((5,5)))))
x() # works
torch.compile(x, backend="eager")() # crashes
```

```
OutputGraph.call_user_compiler   0.0042
```

### Minified repro

```python
import torch
from torch_geometric.nn.module_dict import ModuleDict

edge_type = ("a", "to", "b")

class SomeModel(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.module_dict = ModuleDict({
            edge_type: torch.nn.Linear(1, 1),
        })

    def forward(self, x):
        key = ModuleDict.to_internal_key(edge_type)
        x = self.module_dict[key](x)
        return x

# (1) shows that module has graph breaks
from torch._dynamo.utils import CompileProfiler
with CompileProfiler() as prof:
    model = torch.compile(SomeModel())
    model(torch.randn(1, 1))
    print(prof.report())

# (2) shows that module has NO graph break
model = SomeModel()
explain = torch._dynamo.explain(model)(torch.randn(1, 1))
print(explain)
```

For the note, https://github.com/pyg-team/pytorch_geometric/commit/40cc3b1b8e13dbe66a77bcf6bbd9f939a2d8fa27 was used to reproduce this.

issue_info.title: optim direction tests fail on dynamo due to it specializing on the init_value

labels.name: module: optimizer
labels.description: Related to torch.optim

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

I spent quite a while trying to minimize the test to a repro but failed :/ The best repro is:

1. Remove the potential skip for test_param_group_with_lrscheduler_goes_right_direction in common_optimizers.py under SGD/Adam

2. Run the test:
```
PYTORCH_TEST_WITH_DYNAMO=1  python test/test_optim.py -k test_param_group_with_lrscheduler_goes_right_direction_SGD
```

The reason SGD and Adam fail (and not the others) is because they both run for more than one iteration within the test.

BTW I suspect this is why the other tests still fail even though the closure issue is complete cc @vincentqb @jbschlosser @albanD @crcrpar @ezyang @msaroufim @bdhirsh @anijain2305 @chauhang @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @kadeng @mlazos 


### Error logs

```
======================================================================
FAIL: test_param_group_with_lrscheduler_goes_right_direction_SGD_cuda_float32 (__main__.TestOptimRenewedCUDA)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/data/users/janeyx/pytorch/torch/testing/_internal/common_utils.py", line 2741, in wrapper
    method(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/data/users/janeyx/pytorch/torch/testing/_internal/common_utils.py", line 2741, in wrapper
    method(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/data/users/janeyx/pytorch/torch/testing/_internal/common_device_type.py", line 419, in instantiated_test
    result = test(self, **param_kwargs)
  File "/data/users/janeyx/pytorch/torch/testing/_internal/common_optimizers.py", line 206, in test_wrapper
    return test(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/data/users/janeyx/pytorch/test/test_optim.py", line 199, in test_param_group_with_lrscheduler_goes_right_direction
    self.assertLess(closure().item(), initial_value)
AssertionError: 10.20158863067627 not less than 10.20158863067627

To execute this test, run the following from the base repo dir:
    PYTORCH_TEST_WITH_DYNAMO=1 python test/test_optim.py -k test_param_group_with_lrscheduler_goes_right_direction_SGD_cuda_float32

This message can be suppressed by setting PYTORCH_PRINT_REPRO_ON_FAILURE=0

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 2 tests in 22.902s

FAILED (failures=2)
```

### Minified repro

The test looks like:
```
@optims(optim_db, dtypes=[torch.float32])
    def test_param_group_with_lrscheduler_goes_right_direction(self, device, dtype, optim_info):
        optim_cls = optim_info.optim_cls

        for schedulers_c in optim_info.scheduler_inputs:
            weight = Parameter(torch.randn((10, 5), device=device, dtype=dtype))
            bias = Parameter(torch.randn((10), device=device, dtype=dtype))
            inpt = torch.randn(5, device=device, dtype=dtype)

            optimizer = optim_cls([{"params": [weight]}, {"params": [bias], "lr": 0.01}])
            schedulers = [scheduler_c(optimizer) for scheduler_c in schedulers_c]

            def closure():
                optimizer.zero_grad()
                loss = (weight.mv(inpt) + bias).pow(2).sum()
                loss.backward()
                if optim_info.only_supports_sparse_grads:
                    # For this test, we naively convert the Tensor layout, which we know does
                    # NOT represent the expected use case for optims like SparseAdam!
                    weight.grad = weight.grad.to_sparse()
                    bias.grad = bias.grad.to_sparse()
                return loss

			>>> WHAT I THINK IS WRONG: every iteration after the first, initial_value is somehow the last iteration's final value
            initial_value = closure().item()    
            >>> end.

            for _ in range(20):
                loss = optimizer.step(closure)
                for scheduler in schedulers:
                    if isinstance(scheduler, ReduceLROnPlateau):
                        scheduler.step(loss)
                    else:
                        scheduler.step()

            self.assertLess(closure().item(), initial_value)
```

At the same time, I tried minimizing to something to that effect but the following script does not fail! Confused. 
```
import torch

@torch.compile(backend="eager")
def f():
    for _ in range(4):
        i = torch.randn(2, 3) # torch.tensor([[400], [500]], dtype=torch.float32)
        j = torch.randn(2, 3) # torch.tensor([[200], [300]], dtype=torch.float32)
        def c():
            print("graph break")
            loss = (i + j).sum()
            return loss
        init_val = c().item()
        def s():
            print("step")
            i.sub_(1)
            j.sub_(1)
        for _ in range(3):
            c()
            s()
        assert c().item() < init_val

    f()
```

### Versions

on main

issue_info.title: [dynamo] torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: dtype must be a torch.dtype (got type)

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: module: compiled autograd
labels.description: compiled_autograd

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

got errors for the following case with compiled_autograd.

```
import torch
from torch._dynamo.utils import maybe_enable_compiled_autograd
from torch.cuda.amp import custom_bwd, custom_fwd


class CustomOp(torch.autograd.Function):
	@staticmethod
	@custom_fwd
	def forward(ctx, i):
		result = i @ i
		torch._dynamo.graph_break() 
		return result

	@staticmethod
	@custom_bwd
	def backward(ctx, grad_output):
		return grad_output * 2.0


def custom_function(x):
	x = CustomOp.apply(x)
	return x



def fn(x):
	x = x * x
	y = custom_function(x)
	res = x @ y

	return res


x = torch.randn((1000, 1000), dtype=torch.float32, device="cpu").requires_grad_(True)

with torch.autocast(dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_type="cpu", enabled=True):
	with maybe_enable_compiled_autograd(True):
		fn = torch.compile(fn)
		r = fn(x)
		l = torch.sum(r)
		l.backward()


```

### Error logs

torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: dtype must be a torch.dtype (got type)

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

```
def config_model(self):
    flags = self.FLAGS.MODEL
    model = self.get_model(flags)
    model.cuda(device=self.device)
    if self.world_size > 1:
      if flags.sync_bn:
        model = torch.nn.SyncBatchNorm.convert_sync_batchnorm(model)
      model = torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel(
          module=model, device_ids=[self.device],
          output_device=self.device, broadcast_buffers=False,
          find_unused_parameters=flags.find_unused_parameters, static_graph=False)
    if self.is_master:
      print(model)
      if flags.use_compile:
        print('compile the model...')
        model = torch.compile(model)
        print('compiled successfully')
    self.model = model
```

issue_info.title: Calling id function on model param causes error or graph break for compile

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

When compiling a model, if built-in id function on the parameter Tensor is used in model code, it will cause an error if full_graph=True and it will cause graph breaks for full_graph=False. We saw this problem in DeepSpeed Stage 1 and 2 optimizer when running DeepSpeed with compiled autograd.

The following is the example demonstrate this problem (Of course, DeepSpeed is using similar logic in optimizer code instead model forward code):

```
import torch

class M(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        # constant tensors (similar to the model parameters such as weights)
        self.y1 = torch.ones(2)
        self.y2 = torch.zeros(2)
        
        self.ref_y1_id = id(self.y1)
        self.ref_y2_id = id(self.y2)
    
    def forward(self, x, ref_id):
        if ref_id == id(self.y1):
            x = torch.mul(x, self.y1)
        else:
            x = torch.mul(x, self.y2)
        return x


x = torch.ones(2)

m1 = M()
compiled_m1 = torch.compile(m1, fullgraph=True)

prediction = compiled_m1(x, m1.ref_y1_id)
prediction = compiled_m1(x, m1.ref_y2_id)
```
The above code will show the following error:

```
  File "/home/haifchen/working/envs/env_tensor_id/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/variables/builtin.py", line 1520, in call_id
    unimplemented(f"call_id with args {args}")
  File "/home/haifchen/working/envs/env_tensor_id/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/exc.py", line 190, in unimplemented
    raise Unsupported(msg)
torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported: call_id with args (TensorVariable(),)

from user code:
   File "/home/haifchen/working/test/test-tensor-id.py", line 94, in forward
    if ref_id == id(self.y1):
```

The basic thing it is doing is it call id function on param and use the resulted id to identify a param. Although we can use directly the self.ref_y1_id to replace  id(self.y1) easily in the above code. While for many optimizers that was written, the calling path and code is quite complex and to refactor the code to use the id value initialized at the beginning is not a trivial work.

It is necessary to support built-in id function call on parameters (tensors) in such use cases, so that user model code or optimizer code doesn't need to change to support compile mode or compiled autograd without graph breaks.

If it can be done, it is a great user experience improvement for enabling torch compile or compiled autograd.

### Versions

issue_info.title: dynamo creates unnecessary buffers

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: internal ramp-up task
labels.description: Tasks that are suitable for new folks w/ high-touch guidance from senior PyTorch folks

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Following creates two triton kernels instead of one. In fact the `buf0` is unused.
 
```python
import torch, itertools

n = 200
a = torch.randn((n, n, n), device='cuda')

def fn(a):
    t = (a, a + 1, a + 2)
    shape = a.shape
    m = 1
    for dim in range(len(shape)):
        view_shape = [1]*(dim + 1)
        view_shape[dim] = -1
        b = (torch.arange(shape[dim], device=a.device).view(view_shape))
        m = torch.mul(m, b)
    return sum(torch.mul(t1, m) for t1 in t)
    
torch.compile(fn)(a);
```

<details>


issue_info.title: torch.compile graph break due to unsupported builtin filter function

labels.name: feature
labels.description: A request for a proper, new feature.

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

torch.compile breaks graph with unsupported filter function for the following example:

```
import torch
import torch._dynamo as dynamo
 
def toy_example(inputs):
    out = inputs[0]
    for inp in filter(lambda x: (x.requires_grad), inputs):
      out = out * inp
    out = out + 2000
    return out
 
input1 = torch.arange(2, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
input2 = torch.arange(2, dtype=torch.bfloat16).requires_grad_(True)
 
compiled_fn = torch.compile(toy_example, backend="inductor")
 
r1 = compiled_fn([input1, input2])
print("r1 = ", r1.cpu())
```

This causes a graph break -

issue_info.title: torch.Tensor.random_ causes invalid syntax in InternalTorchDynamoError

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: dynamo-must-fix
labels.description: These bugs affect TorchDynamo reliability.

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

The op signature from https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.Tensor.random_.html#torch.Tensor.random_ 
is that first argument is from and second to, looks like this is causing trouble in tracing

```
    exec(compile(src, key, "exec"), globals)
torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: invalid syntax (<eval_with_key>.0, line 6)
```

### Minified repro

```
import torch


def random_op(tensor, params):
    res = tensor.random_(**params)
    return res


if __name__ == "__main__":
    random_op = torch.compile(random_op)
    params = {"from": -10, "to": 10}
    tensor = torch.randn([2, 3])
    res = random_op(tensor, params)
    print(res)
```

```python
import torch

    return None, None

compile_repro(input)
```

issue_info.title: torch.Tensor.uniform_ causes invalid syntax in InternalTorchDynamoError

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

This issue is a follow-up to https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/121621 . Basically the same issue, different op.
The op signature from https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.Tensor.uniform_.html#torch.Tensor.uniform_
is that first argument is from and second to, looks like this is causing trouble in tracing.

issue_info.title: AssertionError: Dict types must use ConstDictVariable
issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

I am trying to execute following code
```
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

@torch.compile(backend="inductor")
def test_gpt2_demo():
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").to(torch.float)
    prompt = "Thanks for"
    print("\nInput prompt: ", prompt, "\n")

    # run on CPU
    input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids
    gen_tokens = model.generate(
        input_ids,
        max_length=4,
        do_sample=False,
        pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
    )
    gen_text = tokenizer.batch_decode(gen_tokens)[0]

    print("CPU output: ", gen_text, "\n")

test_gpt2_demo()
```                
However, I am getting following error.
```
  File "/local/mnt/workspace/users/vpandya/pytorch-upstream/install/local/lib/python3.11/dist-packages/torch/_dynamo/variables/constant.py", line 44, in create
    assert not isinstance(value, disallowed_type), reason
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
AssertionError: Dict types must use ConstDictVariable.
```

issue_info.title: [regression] Incorrect symbolic output shape and guards for arange, avg pool and conv ops

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

When using latest PyTorch 2.6 RC, it looks like the output shape metadata in the compile dynamic flow is incorrect for the arange OP.
I received the following graph, where an output shape is calculated as: (s0 + 1//2) where in PT 2.5 it is: ((s0 + 1)//2). 

PT 2.6 graph:
```
TRACED GRAPH
 ===== __compiled_fn_3 =====
 .../site-packages/torch/fx/_lazy_graph_module.py class GraphModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def forward(self, L_end_: "Sym(s0)"):
        l_end_ = L_end_
         # File: .../repro.py:11 in fn, code: return torch.arange(start=0, step=2, end=end, device=device)
        arange: "i64[(s0 + 1//2)][1]cpu" = torch.arange(start = 0, step = 2, end = l_end_, device = 'cpu');  l_end_ = None
        return (arange,)
```
The issue is observed since this PR has been merged: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/140597 
Also it was observed for another OPs like AvgPool or Conv.

However, with below reproducer I don't get any explicit error or incorrect results.

Reproducer
```
import torch
import logging

torch._logging.set_logs(dynamo = logging.DEBUG)
torch._dynamo.reset()

device, backend = 'cpu', 'eager'

def fn(end):
    return torch.arange(start=0, step=2, end=end, device=device)

fn_cmp = torch.compile(fn, dynamic=None, fullgraph=True, backend=backend)

for end in [7,17,13]:
    res = fn_cmp(end)
    print(res)
```
CC
@isuruf @anijain2305 @ezyang 


### Error logs

issue_info.title: NestedUserFunctionVariable not define function python_type()

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:

while torch.compile a function which contains nested function, throw "NestedUserFunctionVariable() has no type"
caused by "validate_args_and_maybe_create_graph_inputs" trying to access "a.python_type()".

issue_info.title: [torch.compile] AssertionError: expected FunctionType found property when compiling model with PyTorch and TensorRT

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

I encountered an `AssertionError` when attempting to compile a model using Torch-TensorRT with PyTorch. The error occurs when using a `@property` within the model's forward pass. The specific error message is:

```
AssertionError: expected FunctionType found property <property object at 0x7fd7144351d0>
```

The error traceback suggests that the issue arises from using the `POINT` property of the `BoundingBox2DIndex` class during the forward pass.

This issue was first encountered when compiling a real-world model based on the [Transfuser model](https://github.com/autonomousvision/navsim/blob/main/navsim/agents/transfuser/transfuser_model.py#L139) from the NAVSIM project. The model's forward pass includes operations involving `@property` in an Class, which appear to trigger the error.

The following simplified code can reproduce the issue:

### Reproduction Code:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch_tensorrt
from enum import IntEnum

class BoundingBox2DIndex(IntEnum):
    _X = 0
    _Y = 1
    _HEADING = 2
    _LENGTH = 3
    _WIDTH = 4

    @classmethod
    def size(cls):
        return 5

    @classmethod
    @property
    def X(cls):
        return cls._X

    @classmethod
    @property
    def Y(cls):
        return cls._Y

    @classmethod
    @property
    def HEADING(cls):
        return cls._HEADING

    @classmethod
    @property
    def LENGTH(cls):
        return cls._LENGTH

    @classmethod
    @property
    def WIDTH(cls):
        return cls._WIDTH

    @classmethod
    @property
    def POINT(cls):
        # assumes X, Y have subsequent indices
        return slice(cls._X, cls._Y + 1)

    @classmethod
    @property
    def STATE_SE2(cls):
        # assumes X, Y, HEADING have subsequent indices
        return slice(cls._X, cls._HEADING + 1)
    

class SimpleModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(SimpleModel, self).__init__()
        self._mlp_states = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(10, 20),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(20, BoundingBox2DIndex.size()),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        agent_states = self._mlp_states(x)
        agent_states[..., BoundingBox2DIndex.POINT] = (
            agent_states[..., BoundingBox2DIndex.POINT].tanh() * 32
        )
        agent_states[..., BoundingBox2DIndex.HEADING] = (
            agent_states[..., BoundingBox2DIndex.HEADING].tanh() * torch.pi
        )
        return agent_states

# Instantiate the model and prepare input
model = SimpleModel().cuda()
input_tensor = torch.randn(1, 10, dtype=torch.float32).cuda()

# Configure TensorRT options
enabled_precisions = {torch.float16, torch.float32}

compilation_kwargs = {
    "enabled_precisions": enabled_precisions,
    "debug": True,
    "dryrun": True,
    "workspace_size": 20 << 30,
    "min_block_size": 5,
    "torch_executed_ops": {},
}

# Compile the model with TensorRT
trt_mod = torch.compile(model.eval(), backend="tensorrt", options=compilation_kwargs)
out_trt = trt_mod(input_tensor)
print(out_trt)
```

### Additional Context:
- This issue was first noticed while compiling a model in active use, specifically the Transfuser model from the NAVSIM project. The simplified code provided mirrors the operations in that model to reproduce the error.
- This issue was encountered while testing with both FP16 and FP32 precision settings.

Would appreciate any insights or fixes for this issue.

### Error logs

$  slice_issue.py --precision fp32 
WARNING:torch_tensorrt.dynamo.conversion.aten_ops_converters:Unable to import quantization op. Please install modelopt library (https://github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT-Model-Optimizer?tab=readme-ov-file#installation) to add support for compiling quantized models
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/hoonkyungc/miniconda3/envs/navsim_nv/lib/python3.9/runpy.py", line 197, in _run_module_as_main
    return _run_code(code, main_globals, None,
  File "/home/hoonkyungc/miniconda3/envs/navsim_nv/lib/python3.9/runpy.py", line 87, in _run_code
    exec(code, run_globals)
  File "/home/hoonkyungc/.vscode/extensions/ms-python.debugpy-2024.8.0-linux-x64/bundled/libs/debugpy/adapter/../../debugpy/launcher/../../debugpy/__main__.py", line 39, in <module>
    cli.main()
  File "/home/hoonkyungc/.vscode/extensions/ms-python.debugpy-2024.8.0-linux-x64/bundled/libs/debugpy/adapter/../../debugpy/launcher/../../debugpy/../debugpy/server/cli.py", line 430, in main
    run()
  File "/home/hoonkyungc/.vscode/extensions/ms-python.debugpy-2024.8.0-linux-x64/bundled/libs/debugpy/adapter/../../debugpy/launcher/../../debugpy/../debugpy/server/cli.py", line 284, in run_file
    runpy.run_path(target, run_name="__main__")

issue_info.title: torch.compile is not working with torchaudio.functional.lfilter

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: module: pt2-dispatcher
labels.description: PT2  dispatcher-related issues (e.g., aotdispatch, functionalization, faketensor, custom-op,

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

The new `torch.compile` feature does not work with `torchaudio.functional.lfilter`. My understanding is that `torch.compile` needs to know the shapes of the tensor, but these shapes are not fixed as the filter is applied recursively to any possible waveform.

Note: I already posted this issue here: https://github.com/pytorch/audio/issues/3709 but apparently the `torchaudio` project no longer has an active maintainer...

___

Minimal error code:
```python
import os
import torch
import torchaudio

# Set the environment variables to enable more information in error logs
os.environ["TORCH_LOGS"] = "+dynamo"
os.environ["TORCHDYNAMO_VERBOSE"] = "1"

# Define some tensors
a = torch.Tensor((1, 0))
b = torch.Tensor((1, 0))
waveform = torch.ones((1, 1, 16000))


# Define the function to compile
def my_lfilter(x):
    return torchaudio.functional.lfilter(x, a, b)


# Classic non compiled filtering
filtered_waveform = my_lfilter(waveform)

# Compiling the function, this is passing
my_lfilter_compiled = torch.compile(my_lfilter)

# The error is here, when calling the compiled function
filtered_waveform_compiled = my_lfilter_compiled(waveform)



```
___


## Repro:
```
# all-miniLM
# https://huggingface.co/sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import time

#Mean Pooling - Take attention mask into account for correct averaging
def mean_pooling(model_output, attention_mask):
    token_embeddings = model_output[0] #First element of model_output contains all token embeddings
    input_mask_expanded = attention_mask.unsqueeze(-1).expand(token_embeddings.size()).float()
    return torch.sum(token_embeddings * input_mask_expanded, 1) / torch.clamp(input_mask_expanded.sum(1), min=1e-9)


# Sentences we want sentence embeddings for
sentences = ['This is an example sentence', 'Each sentence is converted']

# Load model from HuggingFace Hub
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained('sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2')

# Tokenize sentences
encoded_input = tokenizer(sentences, padding=True, truncation=True, return_tensors='pt')

@torch.compile(fullgraph=True)
def do_work():
    # Compute token embeddings
    with torch.no_grad():
        model_output = model(**encoded_input)

    # Perform pooling
    sentence_embeddings = mean_pooling(model_output, encoded_input['attention_mask'])

    # Normalize embeddings
    sentence_embeddings = F.normalize(sentence_embeddings, p=2, dim=1)

do_work()
```

```
You can suppress this exception and fall back to eager by setting:

    torch._dynamo.config.suppress_errors = True
```

### Minified repro


```
import torch


class CustomOp(torch.autograd.Function):
	@staticmethod
	def forward(ctx, i):
		result = i + i
		tmp = (result, i)
		ctx.cached_data = tmp
		return result

	@staticmethod
	def backward(ctx, grad_output):
		for t in ctx.cached_data:
			if hasattr(t, 'saved_data'):
				result = grad_output * t
			else:
				result = grad_output * 2
		ctx.cached_data = None
		return result


@torch.compile(backend='inductor', fullgraph=True) 
def custom_function(x):
	x = CustomOp.apply(x)
	return x


def main():
    a = torch.tensor([1., 2.]).requires_grad_(True)
    r = custom_function(a)
    l = torch.sum(r)
    l.backward(torch.tensor(1.))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()


```

issue_info.title: torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: DeviceMeshVariable() has no type

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: module: dtensor
labels.description: distributed tensor tag

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Sharding and compiling a single Linear module works well, but trying to register an all-gather forward hook on it seems to break (edit: to be clear, I think the error is related to Dynamo trying to compile the collective for this device mesh, not the hook itself). ~~Is this an unexpected way of using `network.linear.register_forward_hook` or a bug?~~

Here's a toy repro and the log:

```
import torch
from torch.distributed._tensor import DeviceMesh
from torch.distributed.tensor.parallel import parallelize_module, ColwiseParallel
from torch.distributed._tensor import Shard, distribute_tensor, Replicate
from torch.distributed import _functional_collectives as funcol

mesh = DeviceMesh("cuda", torch.arange(2))

class Test(torch.nn.Module):
  def __init__(self):
    super(Test, self).__init__()
    self.weight1 = torch.nn.Linear(in_features=128, out_features=128)
  def forward(self, input):
    return self.weight1(input)


class Network(torch.nn.Module):
  def __init__(self, mesh):
    super(Network, self).__init__()
    self.linear = Test()

    self.linear_parallelized = parallelize_module(self.linear, mesh, {"weight1": ColwiseParallel()})
    self.linear_compiled = torch.compile(self.linear_parallelized)

  def forward(self, input):
    out = self.linear_compiled(input)
    return out


network = Network(mesh)
network.linear.register_forward_hook(lambda _module, _input, output: funcol.all_gather_tensor(output, -1, mesh))

input = torch.rand([128, 128], requires_grad=True)
input = distribute_tensor(input, mesh, [Replicate()])
output = network(input)

x = torch.unsqueeze(output, -1)
labels = torch.unsqueeze(torch.randint(0, 128, (128,)), -1).cuda()

loss_func = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
loss = loss_func(x, labels)
loss.backward()
```

comments: Follow up: I can resolve this by adding a `python_type` method to the DeviceMeshVariable class [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/e4e80dc9b3a3b09b3e514617c884ed1df2e4f4c1/torch/_dynamo/variables/distributed.py#L132), e.g. `def python_type(self):; return type(self.value)`, since otherwise it inherits the `NotImplementedError` from `VariableTracker`. Is this sufficient or is there more work needed on `DeviceMeshVariable` to make it compatible?
cc @voznesenskym didn't you have a device mesh pr or something

@mathemakitten orthogonal to this torch.compile issue (I think we should fix this compile issue too).

I'm wondering if you tried using the `ColwiseParallel(input_layouts=Shard(shard_dim))` kwarg instead? This `input_layouts` kwarg would annotate the input sharding be sharded on the `shard_dim` and under the hood it will perform a allgather directly, and it should be torch.compile compatible.

Thanks for the suggestion @wanchaol. 

To be specific, to trigger the all-gather you'd have to also set the `output_layouts` kwarg, right? e.g. `ColwiseParallel(input_layouts=Shard(-1), output_layouts=Replicate())`? Otherwise I think the output would still be sharded at the end, right?

> To be specific, to trigger the all-gather you'd have to also set the `output_layouts` kwarg, right? e.g. `ColwiseParallel(input_layouts=Shard(-1), output_layouts=Replicate())`? Otherwise I think the output would still be sharded at the end, right?

No, `output_layouts` mean the module output sharding layout, it's AFTER the execution of the module. `ColwiseParallel` desired input layout is Replicate() so you only need to specify `input_layouts` to trigger allgather on the inputs BEFORE the module execution.  The default output sharding layout of `ColwiseParallel` is shard on the last dimension, if you want to control the output sharding, you can specify it as replicate, where AFTER the nn.Linear/Embedding execution it would trigger another allgather

@mathemakitten sorry this is the new UX we introduced recently so the doc might not be documented enough about what happened under the hood, there is some [updated example of sequence parallelism with the swiglu MLP](https://github.com/pytorch/examples/blob/main/distributed/tensor_parallelism/sequence_parallel_example.py). This would trigger one allgather in the beginning of the first Colwise linear layer, and a reduce_scatter in the end after the last Rowwise linear layer, as the sharding layout conversion become: Shard(0) -> Replicate() means allgather and Partial() -> Shard(0) means reduce_scatter

Thanks for the clarification! I think this is consistent — originally I wanted to all-gather after module execution, but I can do that with model shardings as opposed to a forward hook. The input_layouts makes sense with your example of sequence parallelism. 

When I remove the forward hook in favour of model shardings the DeviceMesh error goes away, so I think we're all good here unless someone _does_ actually want to use a forward hook.
Scrubbing old issues. Per the last update, I'm assuming we can close this out but feel free to reopen if I've misread the conversation.

issue_info.title: [dynamo] Recompilation on a counter-like attribute of nn module

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Repro

~~~
import torch

class Mod(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.counter = 0
        self.linear = torch.nn.Linear(1, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        self.counter += 1
        return self.linear(x)

mod = Mod()
opt_mod = torch.compile(mod, backend="eager")

for _ in range(10):
    opt_mod(torch.randn(1, 1))
~~~

I expect this usecase to be quite common.  Today, there are 4 compilations in total. We could have gone for symbolic right away, but I guess we don't because of over specialization on 0 and 1.

cc @ezyang if it makes sense to do not perform 0/1 specialization on nn module attributes, and directly make any int attribute SymInt.

@yanboliang because I remember you sent a PR for this.

It's relatively simple to get this in one go, but we are changing a heuristic. I opened a PR to check the blast radius. I'll add your test in if we decide we can land it.

issue_info.title: torch.compile + ring attention

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: empathy-day
labels.description: Label for issues from user empathy days

issue_info.body:

issue_info.body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

When I try to compile the ddp + amp model using torch.compile, I get the following error：

```
NotImplementedError: argument of type: <class 'torch.amp.autocast_mode.autocast'>
```

Can you help me with this error?
